subject: Chemistry
title: Bioluminescence: Living Light
description: A 60-second lesson on Bioluminescence: Living Light.

hook.text: Fireflies, deep-sea fish, and glowing mushrooms all make light through the same chemical trick.

buildup.text: A molecule called luciferin reacts with oxygen, aided by an enzyme called luciferase.

discovery.text: The reaction produces an excited molecule that releases energy as a photon of light—with almost no heat.

twist.text: It's the most efficient light production known: nearly 100% of energy becomes light. LEDs can't match it.

climax.text: Scientists use luciferase to track cancer cells and gene activity. Nature's glow lights up medicine.

punchline.text: A chemical reaction that makes cold light. Nature outshines technology.

quiz.question: What makes bioluminescence so efficient?
options: Nearly 100% of energy becomes light with almost no heat
It uses nuclear energy from the organism
It reflects sunlight stored during the day
